samasa.vigraha: asaṃviditaka upādir yasmin, asaṃviditakā ca sthānavijñaptir yasmin

analysis.note: The -ko- is internal sandhi of -ka + upādi; it is not the noun kopa. The commentary construes the compound possessively with yasmin: the ālayavijñāna has an unapprehended upādi and an unapprehended sthānavijñapti.

verification.detail: compound stem asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptika as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Refers to ālayavijñānam, as the commentary explicitly supplies.
The commentary glosses the scope as yāvad ālayavijñānam tāvat: throughout the duration of ālayavijñāna.

The first five members form the list of the five sarvatraga mental factors; vid is glossed by the commentary as vedanā, not as ‘knower’. The compound as a whole is construed like an instrumental tatpuruṣa with anvita.

verification.detail: compound stem sparśamanaskāravitsaṃjñācetanānvita as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: Do not read kopādi as kopa + ādi (‘anger etc.’). Read -ko- as the sandhi of asaṃviditaka + upādi, and let asaṃviditaka qualify both upādi and sthānavijñapti as Sthiramati states.
options_considered: asaṃviditaka + upādi, with asaṃviditakā sthānavijñaptiḥ, as a possessive compound
a-saṃvidita-kopa-ādi-sthāna-vijñaptika, ‘having unrecognized anger etc. and place-presentation’
justifications.chosen: asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptikam is a possessive/bahuvrīhi description of ālayavijñāna: it has an unapprehended upādi and an unapprehended sthānavijñapti.

evidence.quote:
asaṃviditaka upādir yasmin asaṃviditakā ca sthānavijñaptir yasmin tad
ālayavijñānam asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptikam
evidence.translation: ‘That ālaya-consciousness in which the upādi is unapprehended and in which the place-presentation is unapprehended is asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptikam.’

justifications.decision: Use Sthiramati’s upādāna-gloss rather than dictionary guesses such as ‘anger’, ‘affliction’, or a merely spatial ‘basis’.
options_considered: appropriation/taking-up
substrate/basis without the act of appropriation
kopa/anger + ādi as a lexical sequence

evidence.quote:
upādānam upādiḥ
sa punar ātmādivikalpavāsanā rūpādidharmavikalpavāsanā ca
tatsadbhāvād ālayavijñānenātmādivikalpo rūpādivikalpaś ca kāryatvenopātta iti
tad vāsanātmādivikalpānāṃ rūpādivikalpānāñ copādir ity ucyate
evidence.translation: ‘Upādi is upādāna, taking-up/appropriation. It is the latent impression of constructions of self and so on, and the latent impression of constructions of form and so on; because these exist, the constructions of self and form are taken up by ālaya-consciousness as effects. Therefore it is called the upādi of those constructions.’

evidence.quote:
āśrayopādānañ copādiḥ
āśraya ātmabhāvaḥ sādhiṣṭhānam indriyarūpaṃ nāma ca
tasya punar yad upādānam upagamanam ekayogakṣematvena tad upādiḥ
evidence.translation: ‘Appropriation of the basis is also upādi. The basis is the embodied stream, with its seat, material sense-faculties, and name; its taking-up, its entering into a common risk-and-security, is upādi.’

justifications.decision: The verse does not deny that ālayavijñāna functions; it says that its appropriation and place-presentation are not apprehended in the relevant reflective/determinate way.
options_considered: unapprehended/not consciously apprehended
unknown in every possible sense
nonexistent
merely not verbally reported
justifications.chosen: asaṃvidita is rendered ‘unapprehended’ or ‘not consciously apprehended’.

evidence.quote:
tad iti pratisaṃvedanākāreṇāsaṃvidita ity atas tad asaṃviditakopādir ity
ucyate
evidence.translation: ‘Therefore, since it is not apprehended in the mode of reflexive experience as “that”, it is called asaṃviditakopādi.’

evidence.quote:
tat punar upādānam idantayā
pratisaṃvedayitum aśakyam ity ato 'saṃvidita ity ucyate
evidence.translation: ‘That appropriation cannot be reflexively apprehended as “this”; therefore it is called unapprehended.’
‘That too is called unapprehended because it proceeds with an undetermined object-aspect.’

justifications.decision: Do not reduce sthāna to a generic ‘place’ or vijñapti to a bare act of knowing; Sthiramati identifies the compound with bhājanalokasaṃniveśavijñapti.
options_considered: place-presentation of the receptacle-world’s configuration
cognition of a location in general
presentation located in a place
justifications.chosen: sthānavijñapti is translated as ‘place-presentation’, specifically the presentation of the receptacle-world’s configuration.

evidence.translation: ‘The place-presentation is the presentation of the arrangement/configuration of the receptacle-world.’
‘That too is called unapprehended because it proceeds with an undetermined object-aspect.’

justifications.decision: Supply the antecedent ālayavijñāna in the translation; the commentary explicitly makes it the subject of the verse’s predicates.
options_considered: tat as ālayavijñāna from the prior discussion
tat as an unspecified neuter ‘that’ with no supplied antecedent
tat as referring only to the immediately preceding compound

evidence.quote:
asaṃviditaka upādir yasmin asaṃviditakā ca sthānavijñaptir yasmin tad
ālayavijñānam asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptikam
evidence.translation: ‘That ālaya-consciousness in which the upādi is unapprehended and in which the place-presentation is unapprehended is asaṃviditakopādisthānavijñaptikam.’

evidence.quote:
tatrālayākhyaṃ vijñānam
ity uktaṃ vijñānañ cāvaśyaṃ caittaiḥ saṃprayuktam ity ato vaktavyaṃ katamaiḥ
katibhiś ca tebhyaḥ saṃprayujyate
tathā kiṃ taiḥ sarvadā saṃprayujyate
uta
nety ata āha
sadā sparśamanaskāravitsaṃjñācetanānvitam
evidence.translation: ‘Here the consciousness called ālaya has been stated. Since consciousness is necessarily associated with mental factors, it must be said with which and with how many it is associated, and whether it is always associated with them or not; therefore he says: “always accompanied by contact, attention, feeling, recognition, and volition.”’

justifications.decision: Render ‘always’ with its commentary-given scope, and take the final compound as answering which caittas accompany ālayavijñāna.
options_considered: always throughout ālayavijñāna and accompanied by five sarvatraga dharmas
always in an unrestricted metaphysical sense
occasionally associated with these factors
justifications.chosen: sadā means throughout the duration of ālayavijñāna’s occurrence; anvitam means accompanied/associated with five universal mental factors.

evidence.quote:
sadeti yāvad ālayavijñānaṃ tāvad ebhiḥ
sparśamanaskāravedanāsaṃjñācetanākhyaiḥ pañcabhiḥ sarvatragair dharmair
anvitam
evidence.translation: ‘“Always” means: for as long as there is ālaya-consciousness, for that long it is accompanied by these five universal factors called contact, attention, feeling, recognition, and volition.’

evidence.quote:
tathā hi
sadāsparśamanaskāravitsaṃjñācetanānvitam
iti prathamato nirdiṣṭāḥ
sparśa eṣām ādir iti sparśādayaḥ
evidence.translation: ‘For thus, “always accompanied by contact, attention, feeling, recognition, and volition”: they were first stated; since contact is their first, they are “contact and the rest.”’

justifications.decision: Translate the member vit by the technical factor vedanā, because Sthiramati explicitly equates them and then discusses which of the three vedanās is meant.
options_considered: feeling/vedanā
knowledge
knower

evidence.quote:
tatrālayavijñāne vid iti
sāmānyopadeśena na vijñāyate tisṝṇāṃ vedanānāṃ katamā vedanā
evidence.translation: ‘Here, in the case of ālaya-consciousness, because “vid” is a general statement, it is not known which of the three feelings is the feeling meant.’

evidence.quote:
vedanā anubhavasvabhāvā
sā punar
viṣayasyāhlādakaparitāpakatadubhayākāraviviktasvarūpasākṣātkaraṇabhedāt tridhā
bhavati
sukhā
duḥkhā
aduḥkhāsukhā ca
evidence.translation: ‘Feeling has the nature of experience. It is threefold according to the direct realization of the object’s pleasant, painful, or neither-of-the-two character: pleasant, painful, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant.’

justifications.decision: Use English terms guided by the bhāṣya’s functional definitions of the mental factors, not merely by broad dictionary meanings.
options_considered: technical mental-factor renderings based on Sthiramati’s definitions
ordinary physical touch / ordinary attention / name / consciousness without technical restriction
justifications.chosen: The five terms are rendered as contact (sparśa), attention (manaskāra), feeling (vedanā), recognition (saṃjñā), and volition (cetanā).

evidence.quote:
tatra sparśas trikasaṃnipāte indriyavikāraparicchedaḥ
vedanāsaṃniśrayadānakarmakaḥ
indriyaviṣayavijñānāni trīṇy eva trikam
tasya
kāryakāraṇabhāvena samavasthānaṃ trikasaṃnipātaḥ
tasmin sati tatsamakālam
viṣayasya sukhādivedanīyākāraparicchedo yaḥ sa sparśaḥ
evidence.translation: ‘Contact, when the triad has come together, is the discernment of a modification of the faculty and has the function of providing the support for feeling. The triad is precisely faculty, object, and consciousness; their standing together as cause and effect is the conjunction of the triad. When that occurs, the contemporaneous discernment of the object’s character as to-be-felt as pleasant and so on is contact.’

evidence.quote:
manaskāraś cetasa ābhogaḥ
ābhujanam ābhogaḥ
ālambane yena cittam
abhimukhīkriyate
sa punar ālambane cittadhāraṇakarmakaḥ
cittadhāraṇaṃ
punas tatraivālambane punaḥ punaś cittasyāvarjanam
evidence.translation: ‘Attention is the mind’s turning-toward; turning is that by which the mind is made face-to-face with the object. It has the function of holding the mind on the object, that is, repeatedly drawing the mind back to that very object.’

evidence.quote:
saṃjñā viṣayanimittodgrahaṇam
viṣaya ālambanam
nimittaṃ tadviśeṣo
nīlapītādyālambanavyavasthākāraṇam
tasyodgrahaṇaṃ nirūpaṇaṃ nīlam etan na
pītam iti
evidence.translation: ‘Recognition is the grasping of the object’s sign. The object is the ālambana; the sign is its particular feature, the basis for determining an object as blue, yellow, and so on. Grasping it is determination: “this is blue, not yellow.”’

evidence.quote:
cetanā cittābhisaṃskāro manasaś ceṣṭā yasyāṃ satyām ālambanaṃ prati cetasaḥ
praspanda iva bhavaty ayaskāntavaśād ayaḥpraspandavat
evidence.translation: ‘Volition is the mind’s conditioning activity, the movement of manas: when it is present, there is as it were a stirring of the mind toward the object, like the stirring of iron under the power of a magnet.’

translation:
And that storehouse consciousness (ālayavijñāna)
has unapprehended appropriation (upādi)
and an unapprehended place-presentation (sthānavijñapti);
for as long as it is, it is accompanied
by contact (sparśa), attention (manaskāra), feeling (vedanā),
recognition (saṃjñā), and volition (cetanā).
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation would likely split kopādi as kopa + ādi, yielding ‘anger etc.’; Sthiramati instead requires asaṃviditaka + upādi, with upādi = upādāna.
It might translate vid as ‘knowing’ or ‘knower’; Sthiramati explicitly says vedanā vit, making it the mental factor feeling.
It might take sthānavijñapti as a vague ‘place-cognition’; the bhāṣya identifies it as the presentation of the receptacle-world’s configuration and explains why it is unapprehended.
It might render sadā simply as ‘always’ without scope; the commentary construes it as ‘for as long as ālayavijñāna exists, for that long’ it is accompanied by the five universal factors.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 segmented the first compound as a-saṃvidita-kopa-ādi-sthāna-vijñaptikam. The commentary at trbh 203–205 parses it as asaṃviditaka upādiḥ ... asaṃviditakā sthānavijñaptiḥ, with upādi defined as upādāna; therefore kopa and ādi are not verse members here.
ByT5 treated the privative a and saṃvidita as separate analyzed items. In the verse they are internal to the compound-member asaṃviditaka.

ByT5 treated vid as an independent masculine nominative item meaning ‘knower/knowing’. The commentary states vedanā vit at trbh 242 and later asks which of the three vedanās vid denotes at trbh 302–303, so vid is the vedanā member of the compound.

ByT5’s anvitam_anvita_SAM is overridden: anvitam is neuter nominative singular agreeing with tat/ālayavijñānam, as shown by trbh 203–204 and by the gloss yāvad ālayavijñānaṃ ... anvitam at trbh 239–241.
